topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The afternoon sun cast long, accusing shadows across the worn welcome mat. I was just retrieving the junk mail, a mindless chore, when my fingers brushed against something thick and creamy tucked beneath a flyer for a local bake sale. It was a card, folded precisely, bearing no name, no address, just a crudely drawn, vibrant yellow star. My breath hitched, a small, involuntary catch in my chest. I unfolded it slowly, the paper feeling strangely cool beneath my fingertips. The message inside was a single, looping sentence: "I see you." A cold prickle traced a path down my spine, making the fine hairs on my arms stand rigid. I dropped the card as if it had burned me, my heart beginning a frantic, uneven rhythm against my ribs. I stood there for a full minute, staring at the empty mailbox, the silence of the street suddenly amplifying the thumping in my ears.

[story 2]

It was the sheer absurdity of it that first made me pause. A birthday card, no recipient, no sender. I picked it up, the texture of the cardstock oddly smooth against my palm. My gaze darted around the hallway, as if expecting someone to materialize from behind the sofa. The script inside was elegant, almost calligraphic, and the words—a simple, "Happy day"—felt heavy with an unseen weight. I reread it three times, my jaw tightening. A sudden wave of clamminess washed over me, and I instinctively tucked the card deep into the pocket of my cardigan, pressing it against my thigh. Every sound in the house seemed magnified—the hum of the refrigerator, the distant traffic—all conspiring into a low, pervasive drone that made the air feel thick and hard to draw in. I needed to put it somewhere, anywhere, but my hands felt clumsy, trembling slightly as I tried to decide where to deposit this strange, unsolicited token.

[story 3]

I found the card wedged between the utility bill and a flyer for a garage sale. It was stark white, utterly devoid of ornamentation, save for a single, small, perfectly rendered drawing of a closed door. When I opened it, the paper seemed to absorb the light. The inscription inside read, simply, "Remember this." A strange tension coiled in my stomach, tightening like a wire pulled too taut. I didn't move immediately. Instead, I leaned against the kitchen counter, my gaze fixed on the card as if it held some hidden instruction manual. A deep sense of being observed washed over me, a prickling awareness that I was not alone in the room. I stood up abruptly, knocking a glass slightly askew. My voice, when I finally spoke, came out thin and reedy. "Who sent this?" The question hung in the air, unanswered, echoing the strange stillness that had settled over the room.
